title: First-Episode Psychosis / New-Onset Schizophrenia in a 19-Year-Old College Student with 6 Months of Decline—Persecutory Delusions, Command-Type Auditory Hallucinations, Disorganized Thinking, and Prominent Negative Symptoms: Antipsychotic Therapy and Its Adverse Effects (Acute Dystonia, Akathisia, Pseudoparkinsonism, Tardive Dyskinesia, Metabolic Syndrome), Therapeutic Communication with Psychosis, Safety, and Coordinated Care to Support Adherence and Recovery
subtitle: SCHIZOPHRENIA / FIRST-EPISODE PSYCHOSIS = a chronic psychotic disorder typically emerging in the late teens-to-20s (often earlier in men), preceded by a PRODROME (gradual social withdrawal, declining function/grades, odd beliefs) before the ACTIVE phase. SYMPTOM DOMAINS: POSITIVE symptoms (added experiences) — DELUSIONS (fixed false beliefs, often persecutory/paranoid or referential), HALLUCINATIONS (most commonly AUDITORY — voices; assess specifically for COMMAND hallucinations to harm self/others), DISORGANIZED speech/thinking (loose associations, tangentiality, word salad) and disorganized/bizarre behavior; NEGATIVE symptoms (lost functions — often the MOST DISABLING and most treatment-resistant) — flat/blunted AFFECT, ALOGIA (poverty of speech), AVOLITION (lack of motivation), ANHEDONIA, ASOCIALITY; and COGNITIVE symptoms — impaired attention, working memory, and executive function. Diagnosis requires characteristic symptoms with functional decline over time (≥6 months for schizophrenia; shorter durations are schizophreniform/brief psychotic/first-episode psychosis). A shorter DURATION OF UNTREATED PSYCHOSIS (DUP) predicts better outcomes → EARLY INTERVENTION with Coordinated Specialty Care matters. Always rule out medical/substance causes (toxicology, TSH, etc.). TREATMENT — ANTIPSYCHOTICS are the cornerstone: FIRST-GENERATION/typical (haloperidol, fluphenazine, chlorpromazine; strong D2 blockade → MORE extrapyramidal symptoms) and SECOND-GENERATION/atypical (risperidone, paliperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, aripiprazole, ziprasidone, lurasidone, clozapine; fewer EPS but MORE METABOLIC effects). POSITIVE symptoms respond over days-to-weeks (full effect in weeks); NEGATIVE/cognitive symptoms respond less. ADVERSE EFFECTS the nurse must recognize: EXTRAPYRAMIDAL SYMPTOMS (EPS) — (1) ACUTE DYSTONIA: sudden, sustained muscle spasms (torticollis, oculogyric crisis, trismus, and possibly LARYNGOSPASM = airway emergency), often early/within days → treat URGENTLY with IM/IV ANTICHOLINERGIC (benztropine) or DIPHENHYDRAMINE; (2) AKATHISIA: intense motor RESTLESSNESS/inability to sit still + inner tension (often mistaken for worsening agitation/anxiety — do NOT just increase the antipsychotic) → reduce dose/switch, add a beta-blocker (propranolol); (3) PSEUDOPARKINSONISM: tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, masked facies → anticholinergic/dose adjust; (4) TARDIVE DYSKINESIA (TD): LATE-onset (months-years), repetitive involuntary movements (lip-smacking, tongue protrusion, choreoathetoid limb movements), often IRREVERSIBLE → screen periodically with the AIMS (Abnormal Involuntary Movement Scale), minimize exposure, consider a VMAT2 inhibitor (valbenazine/deutetrabenazine). NEUROLEPTIC MALIGNANT SYNDROME (NMS) — a life-threatening idiosyncratic reaction (hyperthermia, 'lead-pipe' rigidity, altered mental status, autonomic instability, markedly elevated CK) → STOP the antipsychotic + emergency supportive care ± dantrolene/bromocriptine (see [[case-068]]). METABOLIC effects (especially olanzapine + clozapine): WEIGHT GAIN, HYPERGLYCEMIA/new diabetes, DYSLIPIDEMIA → monitor weight/BMI/waist, fasting glucose or HbA1c, lipids, and BP at BASELINE and periodically; counsel diet/activity. Other effects: hyperprolactinemia (risperidone/paliperidone → galactorrhea, amenorrhea, gynecomastia, sexual dysfunction), QTc prolongation (e.g., ziprasidone), sedation, orthostatic hypotension, anticholinergic effects, lowered seizure threshold. CLOZAPINE — the most effective agent for TREATMENT-RESISTANT schizophrenia, but reserved due to AGRANULOCYTOSIS (mandatory ANC monitoring via REMS), myocarditis, seizures, severe constipation/ileus, and sedation. ADHERENCE is the central long-term challenge (nonadherence → relapse + rehospitalization + worse trajectory); LONG-ACTING INJECTABLE (LAI/depot) antipsychotics (paliperidone, risperidone, aripiprazole — given every 2-4 weeks or longer) substantially improve adherence and are increasingly used early. THERAPEUTIC COMMUNICATION with psychosis: do NOT argue with, logically challenge, or play along with/reinforce delusions or hallucinations — instead acknowledge the underlying FEELING, calmly present reality without confrontation ('I understand that feels very real and frightening; I don't hear the voices, but I'm here with you and you're safe'), use clear/simple/consistent communication, build trust with consistent staff, reduce environmental stimulation, and avoid whispering/laughing near a paranoid patient. SAFETY: schizophrenia carries an elevated SUICIDE risk; assess directly for suicidal ideation and for COMMAND hallucinations to harm self/others, and ensure a safe environment. MANAGEMENT OF CARE / CARE COORDINATION (the focus of this case): serious mental illness requires COORDINATED, continuous, multidisciplinary care — psychiatry, therapy (CBT for psychosis), CASE MANAGEMENT/social work, family, and primary care; promote ADHERENCE (psychoeducation, address barriers/insight, consider LAI), arrange COMMUNITY RESOURCES (Coordinated Specialty Care/early-psychosis programs, assertive community treatment, supported education/employment, housing, peer support), ensure smooth TRANSITIONS of care + follow-up, coordinate METABOLIC/physical-health monitoring (people with schizophrenia have markedly reduced life expectancy, largely from cardiovascular/physical illness), and provide FAMILY psychoeducation (reducing high 'expressed emotion'—criticism/hostility/overinvolvement—lowers relapse). NURSING ROLE: assess safety + symptoms, administer/monitor antipsychotics and recognize/treat their adverse effects (especially acute dystonia + distinguishing akathisia from agitation + metabolic monitoring + AIMS), communicate therapeutically, educate patient + family, and coordinate the multidisciplinary, community-linked care that supports adherence and recovery

patient.name: J. Park
patient.code: Full code; decisional capacity currently impaired by acute psychosis (assess per psychiatry); parents involved + supportive; goal = ensure safety, treat the psychosis, build a trusting therapeutic relationship, support adherence, and coordinate recovery-oriented care
patient.admitDate: Brought to psychiatric services by his parents after ~6 months of progressive decline noticed since he started college: withdrawing from friends, dropping classes, neglecting hygiene, and voicing increasingly bizarre PERSECUTORY beliefs (that classmates and 'the government' are monitoring and plotting against him). Over the past 2 weeks he has been responding to voices, has barely slept, stopped eating from the dining hall ('it's poisoned'), and told his mother the voices 'tell him what to do.' On interview he is guarded, with a flat affect, sparse and tangential speech, and clear paranoid delusions and auditory hallucinations. Admitted for safety, evaluation (including ruling out medical/substance causes), and initiation of antipsychotic treatment with coordinated care.
patient.room: Inpatient psychiatric unit; calm, low-stimulation environment; consistent staff assignment; safety assessment ongoing; multidisciplinary team (psychiatry, psychiatric nursing, therapy, case management/social work) + family engaged

vitals.temp: 37.0°C; guarded, hypervigilant, intermittently appears to respond to internal stimuli; flat affect, tangential/sparse speech; expresses persecutory delusions (being monitored/poisoned) and reports voices that 'tell him what to do'; poor sleep + reduced intake × 2 weeks; oriented but with impaired insight; baseline metabolic parameters (weight/BMI, waist, fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipids, BP) + ECG (QTc) + toxicology + TSH obtained before/at antipsychotic initiation

37.0°C; direct, non-judgmental SAFETY assessment — specifically screened for suicidal ideation and COMMAND hallucinations to harm self/others (he denies intent to act but the voices are distressing); calm, consistent therapeutic communication established (acknowledged his fear without arguing the delusions); low-stimulation milieu; organic workup unremarkable (negative toxicology, normal TSH) supporting a primary psychotic disorder; atypical antipsychotic initiated after baseline metabolic labs + ECG

37.0°C; developed sudden NECK TWISTING (torticollis) + upward eye deviation (oculogyric crisis) + jaw tightness — ACUTE DYSTONIA from the antipsychotic (an EPS emergency; monitored for any laryngospasm/airway compromise); treated URGENTLY with IM anticholinergic (benztropine)/diphenhydramine with rapid relief; reassured the frightened patient; dose/agent reviewed with psychiatry; continued therapeutic engagement

37.0°C; reports intense inner restlessness + inability to sit still, pacing constantly = AKATHISIA (a medication effect — NOT worsening psychotic agitation); recognized so the antipsychotic was NOT simply increased; managed by dose adjustment ± propranolol; positive symptoms beginning to lessen (less preoccupied with delusions, voices less prominent); engaging more with staff

36.9°C; positive symptoms improved (delusions less fixed, hallucinations diminished, sleeping + eating), though negative symptoms (flat affect, low motivation) persist (expected, slower to respond); no EPS currently; metabolic monitoring ongoing; adherence planning — psychoeducation + discussion of a LONG-ACTING INJECTABLE antipsychotic to support adherence; family psychoeducation begun (reduce expressed emotion, warning signs, support)

36.8°C; stabilized for discharge to a Coordinated Specialty Care (early-psychosis) program; on a maintenance antipsychotic (LAI arranged to support adherence) + therapy (CBT for psychosis) + case management + supported-education plan; metabolic monitoring schedule set; family educated + engaged; safety plan + warning-sign/relapse-prevention teaching completed; follow-up + community resources arranged

labs.n: PRESENTATION + symptom domains + ruling out mimics
labs.v: 19 y/o male college student J. Park brought by his parents after ~6 MONTHS of progressive decline that began after starting college: a PRODROME of social withdrawal, dropping classes, declining hygiene/function, and increasingly odd/bizarre beliefs, evolving into an ACTIVE psychotic phase. POSITIVE symptoms: fixed PERSECUTORY DELUSIONS (classmates and 'the government' are monitoring/plotting against him; food is 'poisoned'), AUDITORY HALLUCINATIONS including voices that 'tell him what to do' (must specifically assess for COMMAND hallucinations to harm self/others), and DISORGANIZED, tangential, sparse thinking/speech. NEGATIVE symptoms (often most disabling): FLAT/blunted affect, ALOGIA (poverty of speech), AVOLITION (loss of motivation), social withdrawal. Functional decline (left classes, isolated, neglecting self-care) over >6 months supports schizophrenia (vs schizophreniform <6 months). PMH: previously healthy, good student until ~6 months ago; no chronic illness. SUBSTANCE/MEDICAL: must RULE OUT medical and substance-induced causes — toxicology (cannabis/stimulants/hallucinogens), TSH, metabolic panel, and consider other organic causes; his toxicology is negative and TSH normal, supporting a primary psychotic disorder. FAMILY HISTORY: an uncle with a 'nervous breakdown' in his 20s (possible familial loading). SOCIAL: lives in a college dorm (now home with parents); supportive, frightened parents involved; no prior psychiatric treatment; the DURATION OF UNTREATED PSYCHOSIS has been ~weeks-months, and shorter DUP predicts better outcomes — early, coordinated treatment matters. On exam he is guarded, hypervigilant, intermittently responding to internal stimuli, with impaired insight. He is admitted for SAFETY, evaluation (including excluding mimics), antipsychotic initiation, therapeutic engagement, and coordinated, recovery-oriented care. The nurse's first priorities: establish safety + a trusting therapeutic relationship, assess symptoms + risk, and support evaluation/treatment.
labs.r: First-episode psychosis/new-onset schizophrenia: positive (delusions, auditory/command hallucinations, disorganization), negative (flat affect, alogia, avolition), and cognitive symptoms with >6-month functional decline; rule out substance/medical mimics. Shorter duration of untreated psychosis → better outcomes (early coordinated care). RN role: safety + therapeutic relationship + symptom/risk assessment + support evaluation/treatment.

labs.n: Antipsychotic therapy + EPS (acute dystonia, akathisia, pseudoparkinsonism, TD)
labs.v: ANTIPSYCHOTICS are the cornerstone of treatment. FIRST-GENERATION/typical (haloperidol, fluphenazine, chlorpromazine) potently block D2 → effective for positive symptoms but MORE extrapyramidal symptoms; SECOND-GENERATION/atypical (risperidone, paliperidone, olanzapine, quetiapine, aripiprazole, ziprasidone, lurasidone, clozapine) → fewer EPS but MORE metabolic effects, and are commonly first-line. POSITIVE symptoms improve over days-to-weeks (full effect in weeks); NEGATIVE/cognitive symptoms respond less and more slowly — set expectations accordingly. EXTRAPYRAMIDAL SYMPTOMS (EPS) the nurse must recognize/manage: (1) ACUTE DYSTONIA — sudden, sustained, often painful muscle contractions: TORTICOLLIS (neck twisting), OCULOGYRIC CRISIS (forced upward eye deviation), trismus/jaw spasm, opisthotonos, and possibly LARYNGOSPASM (airway emergency); typically EARLY (hours-days); frightening but rapidly reversible → treat URGENTLY with an IM/IV ANTICHOLINERGIC (BENZTROPINE) or DIPHENHYDRAMINE, monitor the airway, and reassure the patient; (2) AKATHISIA — a subjective sense of inner restlessness with an inability to sit still (pacing, shifting); it is COMMONLY MISTAKEN for worsening psychotic agitation/anxiety — the dangerous error is to simply INCREASE the antipsychotic, which worsens it; instead reduce the dose/switch and consider a BETA-BLOCKER (propranolol) ± benzodiazepine; akathisia is distressing and linked to suicidality, so recognize it; (3) PSEUDOPARKINSONISM — tremor, cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia, masked facies, shuffling gait (drug-induced parkinsonism); manage with dose reduction/switch ± anticholinergic; (4) TARDIVE DYSKINESIA (TD) — LATE (months-years of exposure), repetitive INVOLUNTARY movements (lip-smacking, tongue protrusion/'fly-catching,' grimacing, choreoathetoid limb/trunk movements); often IRREVERSIBLE → screen periodically with the AIMS (Abnormal Involuntary Movement Scale), use the lowest effective dose, prefer agents with lower TD risk, and treat with a VMAT2 inhibitor (valbenazine/deutetrabenazine) if it develops. Anticholinergics (benztropine) help dystonia/pseudoparkinsonism but NOT TD (and can worsen TD) and not akathisia. THIS PATIENT developed acute dystonia (torticollis + oculogyric crisis) on day 3 → treated promptly with IM benztropine/diphenhydramine; and akathisia on day 6 → correctly recognized as a medication effect (not increased agitation) and managed by dose adjustment ± propranolol. (Neuroleptic malignant syndrome — hyperthermia, lead-pipe rigidity, altered mentation, autonomic instability, elevated CK — is a separate antipsychotic emergency requiring drug discontinuation + supportive care; see [[case-068]].)
labs.r: Antipsychotics: typical (more EPS) vs atypical (more metabolic). EPS — ACUTE DYSTONIA (torticollis/oculogyric crisis ± laryngospasm; early; treat URGENTLY with IM benztropine/diphenhydramine + monitor airway); AKATHISIA (inner restlessness — do NOT mistake for agitation/increase the dose; use beta-blocker/reduce dose); PSEUDOPARKINSONISM (anticholinergic/dose); TARDIVE DYSKINESIA (late, often irreversible; screen with AIMS; VMAT2 inhibitor). NMS = separate emergency ([[case-068]]). RN role: recognize + treat EPS, distinguish akathisia from agitation.

labs.n: Metabolic monitoring, other adverse effects, clozapine, and adherence/LAIs
labs.v: METABOLIC + OTHER ADVERSE EFFECTS + ADHERENCE are central to safe long-term management: (1) METABOLIC SYNDROME — especially with OLANZAPINE and CLOZAPINE (also quetiapine/risperidone to varying degrees): significant WEIGHT GAIN, HYPERGLYCEMIA/new-onset DIABETES, and DYSLIPIDEMIA → obtain BASELINE and periodic WEIGHT/BMI/WAIST circumference, FASTING GLUCOSE or HbA1c, LIPID panel, and BLOOD PRESSURE; counsel diet/physical activity; this matters enormously because people with schizophrenia have markedly REDUCED LIFE EXPECTANCY driven largely by cardiovascular/metabolic disease, so physical-health monitoring is a core nursing responsibility, not an afterthought. (2) HYPERPROLACTINEMIA (risperidone/paliperidone, and typicals) → galactorrhea, amenorrhea, gynecomastia, sexual dysfunction, and long-term bone-density concerns. (3) QTc PROLONGATION (e.g., ziprasidone, IV haloperidol) → baseline/periodic ECG + caution with other QT-prolonging drugs/electrolyte abnormalities. (4) SEDATION, ORTHOSTATIC HYPOTENSION, ANTICHOLINERGIC effects (dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention), and a LOWERED SEIZURE THRESHOLD. (5) CLOZAPINE — the MOST EFFECTIVE agent for TREATMENT-RESISTANT schizophrenia and for reducing suicidality, but RESERVED because of AGRANULOCYTOSIS (potentially fatal neutropenia) → mandatory regular ABSOLUTE NEUTROPHIL COUNT monitoring through a REMS program; also MYOCARDITIS, SEIZURES, severe CONSTIPATION/ileus, sialorrhea, and sedation — teach patients to report fever/sore throat (infection) and constipation. ADHERENCE is the pivotal long-term issue: nonadherence (from impaired insight, side effects, cost, complexity, substance use, or stigma) is the leading cause of RELAPSE + rehospitalization + deterioration. Strategies: PSYCHOEDUCATION, addressing side effects + barriers, simplifying regimens, involving family/supports, and — increasingly early — LONG-ACTING INJECTABLE (LAI/depot) antipsychotics (e.g., paliperidone, risperidone, aripiprazole given every 2-4 weeks up to every 3-6 months) that remove the need for daily dosing and reduce covert nonadherence. THIS PATIENT: baseline metabolic labs + ECG were obtained before initiation; an atypical was started; metabolic parameters are monitored; and an LAI is planned at discharge to support adherence given a first episode + insight limitations.
labs.r: Monitor metabolic effects (weight/BMI/waist, fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipids, BP at baseline + periodically — esp. olanzapine/clozapine) because cardiometabolic disease drives reduced life expectancy. Other effects: hyperprolactinemia (risperidone/paliperidone), QTc (ziprasidone/IV haloperidol), sedation/orthostasis/anticholinergic. CLOZAPINE = most effective for treatment-resistant but AGRANULOCYTOSIS → ANC/REMS monitoring (+ myocarditis/seizures/constipation). ADHERENCE is key — LAIs reduce relapse. RN role: metabolic + lab monitoring + adherence support + LAI education.

labs.v: THERAPEUTIC COMMUNICATION with a patient experiencing psychosis is a core nursing skill: (1) Do NOT ARGUE with, logically debate, or try to 'talk the patient out of' a DELUSION (arguing entrenches it and damages trust), and do NOT AGREE WITH or 'play along'/reinforce the delusion or hallucination (which validates the false reality); (2) instead, ACKNOWLEDGE the underlying FEELING and gently present reality without confrontation — e.g., 'It sounds terrifying to feel watched; I don't see anyone monitoring you, and I want you to know you're safe here with us,' or for hallucinations, 'I don't hear the voices you're hearing, but I believe that they feel real and frightening to you — what are they saying?' (the last question is important for SAFETY — to assess command hallucinations); (3) focus on the EMOTION/THEME rather than the content; (4) use CLEAR, SIMPLE, CONCRETE language; avoid abstract/figurative speech; one idea at a time; (5) be HONEST and CONSISTENT, with consistent staff assignments to build trust; (6) reduce environmental STIMULATION; avoid WHISPERING or LAUGHING near a paranoid patient (it feeds paranoia); maintain a calm, non-threatening presence and respect personal space; (7) do not make promises you can't keep. SAFETY: schizophrenia carries an ELEVATED SUICIDE RISK (and akathisia/depression add to it) → assess directly and repeatedly for suicidal ideation; assess specifically for COMMAND HALLUCINATIONS to harm self or others and for the patient's intent/ability to resist them; assess for agitation/violence risk and ensure a safe environment + de-escalation; ensure basic needs (the patient who believes food is 'poisoned' may not eat → offer sealed/packaged foods, involve the patient in selection, monitor intake/hydration). The nurse balances honesty about reality with deep respect for the patient's distress, never shaming or dismissing. THIS PATIENT was engaged with calm, consistent communication that acknowledged his fear without reinforcing or arguing the delusions; he was screened directly for command hallucinations and suicide risk; and sealed foods were offered to address the 'poisoning' fear and maintain intake.
labs.r: Therapeutic communication with psychosis: don't argue with OR reinforce delusions/hallucinations — acknowledge the feeling + calmly present reality ('I don't hear the voices, but I know they feel real; you're safe'); ask what the voices say (assess COMMAND hallucinations); clear/simple/consistent language + consistent staff + low stimulation; don't whisper/laugh near a paranoid patient. SAFETY: elevated suicide risk + assess command hallucinations + ensure intake (offer sealed foods). RN role: therapeutic communication + safety.

labs.n: Coordinated care, family, recovery; Pertinent NEGATIVES + protective factors
labs.v: MANAGEMENT OF CARE / CARE COORDINATION is decisive in serious mental illness: schizophrenia is a chronic, relapsing condition whose outcomes depend on CONTINUOUS, COORDINATED, multidisciplinary, recovery-oriented care, not a single hospitalization. The nurse helps coordinate: PSYCHIATRY (medication management), PSYCHOTHERAPY (CBT for psychosis; skills training), CASE MANAGEMENT/SOCIAL WORK, PRIMARY CARE (for the metabolic/physical-health monitoring that drives survival), and FAMILY. Promote ADHERENCE through psychoeducation, addressing barriers/insight, and considering an LAI. Link to COMMUNITY RESOURCES — COORDINATED SPECIALTY CARE / early-psychosis programs (which improve first-episode outcomes), ASSERTIVE COMMUNITY TREATMENT for high-need patients, SUPPORTED EDUCATION/EMPLOYMENT, housing/financial support, and PEER SUPPORT. Ensure smooth TRANSITIONS of care with timely follow-up + a clear, written plan + relapse-prevention/warning-sign teaching + a crisis plan. FAMILY PSYCHOEDUCATION is high-impact: teaching families about the illness and reducing high EXPRESSED EMOTION (criticism, hostility, emotional over-involvement) measurably LOWERS relapse; involve and support the family while respecting the patient's preferences/privacy. PERTINENT NEGATIVES: organic/substance causes were EXCLUDED (negative toxicology, normal TSH) before settling on a primary psychotic disorder; akathisia was NOT mistaken for psychotic agitation (so the antipsychotic was not inappropriately increased); the nurse did NOT argue with or reinforce the delusions; the patient was NOT shamed or dismissed; basic needs (nutrition/hydration despite the 'poisoning' delusion) were NOT neglected; NO neuroleptic malignant syndrome (no hyperthermia/lead-pipe rigidity/elevated CK — distinct emergency, see [[case-068]]); NO untreated acute dystonia/airway compromise (recognized + treated promptly); baseline metabolic labs + ECG were NOT skipped. PROTECTIVE FACTORS: relatively SHORT duration of untreated psychosis with early presentation; SUPPORTIVE, involved family; no comorbid substance use disorder; good premorbid functioning (was a successful student); prompt recognition + treatment of EPS (dystonia, akathisia); a therapeutic alliance built through skilled communication; baseline metabolic monitoring + a plan for ongoing monitoring; an LAI + Coordinated Specialty Care + supported-education plan to sustain adherence and recovery; family psychoeducation to reduce expressed emotion. EXPECTED trajectory: positive symptoms improve with the antipsychotic, negative/cognitive symptoms improve more slowly, and with adherence + coordinated, recovery-oriented care + family support the patient can achieve meaningful functional recovery — while requiring lifelong management + physical-health monitoring. NURSING ROLE: integrate safety, symptom + medication management (including EPS/metabolic), therapeutic communication, patient/family education, and coordinated community-linked care.
labs.r: Care coordination is decisive: multidisciplinary (psychiatry + therapy + case management + primary care + family), promote adherence (psychoeducation + LAI), link to Coordinated Specialty Care/early-psychosis programs + supported education/employment + housing, ensure transitions/follow-up + relapse-prevention, and family psychoeducation (reduce expressed emotion → fewer relapses). Negatives confirm correct practice (mimics excluded, akathisia not mistaken for agitation, didn't argue delusions, dystonia treated, metabolic baseline done). Protective: short DUP + supportive family + early treatment + adherence plan. RN role: integrate safety + meds + communication + education + coordination.

nursesNote: Inpatient psychiatry — 19 y/o male college student J. Park brought by parents after ~6 MONTHS of decline since starting college: prodrome of social withdrawal, dropped classes, poor hygiene/function → active psychosis. POSITIVE symptoms: fixed PERSECUTORY delusions (classmates/'the government' monitoring + plotting; food 'poisoned'), AUDITORY hallucinations including voices that 'tell him what to do,' disorganized/tangential sparse speech. NEGATIVE symptoms: flat affect, alogia, avolition, withdrawal. >6-month functional decline supports schizophrenia. RULED OUT mimics: toxicology negative, TSH normal (also metabolic panel) → primary psychotic disorder. FAMILY HX: uncle with possible psychotic illness in his 20s. SOCIAL: dorm (now home with parents); supportive, frightened parents; no prior psych treatment; good premorbid functioning; no substance use disorder; shorter duration of untreated psychosis → better prognosis with early coordinated care. ADMISSION: guarded, hypervigilant, responding to internal stimuli, impaired insight; VS stable, afebrile. PRIORITIES: safety + therapeutic alliance + evaluation + antipsychotic initiation + coordinated care. Baseline METABOLIC parameters (weight/BMI/waist, fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipids, BP) + ECG (QTc) + toxicology + TSH obtained BEFORE/at antipsychotic initiation. DAY 1: direct, non-judgmental SAFETY assessment — screened specifically for suicidal ideation + COMMAND hallucinations to harm self/others (denies intent to act; voices distressing); established calm, consistent THERAPEUTIC COMMUNICATION — acknowledged his fear WITHOUT arguing or reinforcing the delusions ('I don't hear the voices, but I know they feel real and frightening; you're safe here'); offered SEALED/packaged foods + involved him in selection to address the 'poisoning' fear + maintain intake/hydration; low-stimulation milieu + consistent staff; avoided whispering/laughing near him. Atypical antipsychotic started after baseline labs/ECG. DAY 3: ACUTE DYSTONIA — sudden TORTICOLLIS + OCULOGYRIC CRISIS + jaw tightness (EPS emergency; monitored airway for laryngospasm) → treated URGENTLY with IM benztropine/diphenhydramine with rapid relief + reassurance; agent/dose reviewed with psychiatry. DAY 6: intense inner restlessness + pacing + can't sit still = AKATHISIA (medication effect — correctly NOT mistaken for worsening psychotic agitation, so the antipsychotic was NOT simply increased) → managed by dose adjustment ± propranolol; positive symptoms beginning to lessen. DAY 10: positive symptoms improved (delusions less fixed, hallucinations diminished, sleeping + eating); NEGATIVE symptoms persist (expected, slower); no current EPS; metabolic monitoring ongoing; ADHERENCE planning — psychoeducation + plan for a LONG-ACTING INJECTABLE antipsychotic; FAMILY psychoeducation begun (reduce expressed emotion — criticism/hostility/overinvolvement; warning signs; support). DISCHARGE/transition: stabilized to a COORDINATED SPECIALTY CARE (early-psychosis) program; maintenance antipsychotic (LAI arranged) + CBT for psychosis + case management + supported-education plan; metabolic monitoring schedule set; family educated + engaged; safety plan + relapse-prevention/warning-sign teaching + crisis plan + follow-up + community resources arranged. Throughout: safety, trusting therapeutic relationship, EPS + metabolic vigilance, and coordinated recovery-oriented care.
providerOrders: ADMIT to inpatient psychiatry; first-episode psychosis / new-onset schizophrenia
DIAGNOSIS: first-episode psychosis (rule out substance-induced/medical); schizophrenia-spectrum
CODE STATUS: full code; capacity impaired by acute psychosis — psychiatric capacity/safety evaluation; parents involved
SAFETY:
Assess directly + repeatedly for suicidal ideation and for COMMAND hallucinations to harm self/others; assess violence/agitation risk
Safe environment; de-escalation; calm, low-stimulation milieu; consistent staff assignment
Ensure nutrition/hydration despite 'poisoning' delusion — offer sealed/packaged foods, involve patient in selection, monitor intake
EVALUATION (exclude mimics):
Toxicology, TSH, CBC, comprehensive metabolic panel; consider neuroimaging if atypical/focal findings
Baseline metabolic parameters: weight/BMI, waist circumference, fasting glucose or HbA1c, lipid panel, blood pressure
Baseline ECG (QTc) before/with antipsychotic initiation
ANTIPSYCHOTIC THERAPY:
Initiate an atypical antipsychotic (e.g., risperidone/aripiprazole/olanzapine) per psychiatry; set expectations (positive symptoms improve over days-weeks; negative/cognitive slower)
Monitor + manage extrapyramidal symptoms:
ACUTE DYSTONIA (torticollis/oculogyric crisis/trismus ± laryngospasm) → treat URGENTLY with IM/IV benztropine or diphenhydramine; monitor airway; reassure
AKATHISIA (inner restlessness) → do NOT simply increase the antipsychotic; reduce dose/switch + consider propranolol (± benzodiazepine); distinguish from psychotic agitation
PSEUDOPARKINSONISM (tremor/rigidity/bradykinesia) → dose reduction/switch ± anticholinergic
TARDIVE DYSKINESIA → screen periodically with AIMS; lowest effective dose; VMAT2 inhibitor (valbenazine/deutetrabenazine) if it develops
Monitor for neuroleptic malignant syndrome (hyperthermia, rigidity, altered mentation, autonomic instability, elevated CK) → if suspected, STOP antipsychotic + emergency care (see case-068)
Metabolic monitoring schedule (weight/BMI/waist, glucose/HbA1c, lipids, BP) at baseline + periodically; counsel diet/activity
If treatment-resistant (≥2 adequate trials): consider clozapine with mandatory ANC/REMS monitoring (+ myocarditis/seizure/constipation precautions)
THERAPEUTIC COMMUNICATION:
Do not argue with or reinforce delusions/hallucinations; acknowledge the feeling + calmly present reality; ask what the voices say (assess command hallucinations)
Clear/simple/consistent language; honest; consistent staff; reduce stimulation; avoid whispering/laughing near a paranoid patient
ADHERENCE + CARE COORDINATION:
Psychoeducation on the illness, medications, and side effects; address barriers/insight
Consider a long-acting injectable (LAI) antipsychotic to support adherence (paliperidone/risperidone/aripiprazole)
Coordinate multidisciplinary care: psychiatry + therapy (CBT for psychosis) + case management/social work + primary care (metabolic monitoring) + family
Refer to Coordinated Specialty Care / early-psychosis program; supported education/employment; housing; peer support
FAMILY + DISCHARGE:
Family psychoeducation: the illness, warning signs, supporting adherence, and reducing high expressed emotion (criticism/hostility/overinvolvement)
Relapse-prevention + warning-sign teaching + crisis plan + safety plan
Smooth transition with timely outpatient follow-up + written medication/monitoring plan + community resources

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters in this first-episode psychosis?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: Positive symptoms — persecutory delusions, auditory hallucinations (including voices that 'tell him what to do'), and disorganized thinking — with a 6-month functional decline, consistent with first-episode psychosis/schizophrenia
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the psychotic syndrome + functional decline define the presentation. RN role: assessment + recognition.

opts.text: Safety — assess directly for suicidal ideation (elevated risk in schizophrenia) and specifically for COMMAND hallucinations to harm self or others
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — safety/command-hallucination assessment is essential. RN role: direct risk assessment + safe environment.

opts.text: Negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia, avolition, withdrawal) are present and are often the most disabling and slowest to respond to medication
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — recognizing negative symptoms sets realistic expectations + guides psychosocial care. RN role: assessment + education.

opts.text: Rule out substance-induced and medical causes (toxicology, TSH, metabolic panel) before settling on a primary psychotic disorder
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — mimics must be excluded. RN role: facilitate the workup.

opts.text: Obtain baseline metabolic parameters (weight/BMI/waist, fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipids, BP) and an ECG before/with starting an antipsychotic
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — baseline metabolic + QTc monitoring is core to safe antipsychotic use. RN role: baseline labs/ECG + ongoing monitoring.

opts.text: Anticipate and recognize antipsychotic adverse effects — especially acute dystonia (an EPS emergency) and akathisia (not to be mistaken for agitation)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — EPS recognition/management is critical. RN role: monitor for + treat EPS.

opts.text: The patient is not eating because he believes the food is 'poisoned' — basic needs (nutrition/hydration) must be addressed (e.g., offer sealed/packaged foods)
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — meeting basic needs despite delusions prevents harm. RN role: ensure intake via delusion-sensitive strategies.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation; relevant for medications. Not the central cue.

opts.text: Confront and correct his delusions with logical arguments and proof until he admits they are false
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Arguing with/logically challenging delusions entrenches them and damages trust. Acknowledge the feeling and calmly present reality without confrontation; do not debate the delusion.

opts.text: Treat the patient's pacing and inability to sit still by increasing the antipsychotic dose, assuming it is worsening psychotic agitation
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Restlessness/inability to sit still is likely AKATHISIA (a medication side effect); increasing the antipsychotic worsens it. Recognize akathisia and reduce/switch + consider propranolol.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: Schizophrenia has positive (delusions/hallucinations/disorganization), negative (flat affect/alogia/avolition — often most disabling + treatment-resistant), and cognitive symptom domains; shorter duration of untreated psychosis predicts better outcomes
opts.rat: CORRECT — symptom domains + DUP. Foundation for assessment + early-intervention rationale.

opts.text: Antipsychotics treat positive symptoms (days-weeks) more than negative/cognitive; typicals cause more EPS, atypicals cause more metabolic effects
opts.rat: CORRECT — antipsychotic effect/side-effect framework. Foundation for expectations + monitoring.

opts.text: Acute dystonia (torticollis/oculogyric crisis ± laryngospasm) is an early EPS emergency treated urgently with IM/IV benztropine or diphenhydramine; akathisia (restlessness) is managed by dose reduction/switch + propranolol — not by increasing the antipsychotic
opts.rat: CORRECT — EPS recognition/management. Foundation for safe medication monitoring.

opts.text: Tardive dyskinesia is a late, often irreversible movement disorder → screen with the AIMS, minimize exposure, and treat with a VMAT2 inhibitor; metabolic syndrome (esp. olanzapine/clozapine) requires baseline + periodic weight/glucose/lipids/BP monitoring
opts.rat: CORRECT — TD + metabolic monitoring. Foundation for long-term safety.

opts.text: Therapeutic communication means neither arguing with nor reinforcing delusions/hallucinations — acknowledge the feeling, present reality calmly, ask what the voices say (to assess command hallucinations), and use clear/consistent communication
opts.rat: CORRECT — therapeutic communication + safety principle. Foundation for the alliance + risk assessment.

opts.text: Adherence is the central long-term challenge (nonadherence → relapse); LAIs and coordinated, community-linked care (Coordinated Specialty Care, family psychoeducation to reduce expressed emotion) improve outcomes
opts.rat: CORRECT — adherence + care-coordination principle. Foundation for recovery-oriented management.

opts.text: Clozapine should be used first-line for every new diagnosis of schizophrenia because it is the most effective antipsychotic
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Clozapine is reserved for TREATMENT-RESISTANT schizophrenia (≥2 failed trials) due to agranulocytosis (mandatory ANC/REMS monitoring), myocarditis, seizures, and severe constipation — not first-line.

opts.text: Negative symptoms (flat affect, low motivation) indicate the patient is being deliberately uncooperative and should be confronted to 'try harder'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Negative symptoms are core features of the illness (not willful noncooperation or laziness); confrontation is inappropriate. They are addressed with treatment + psychosocial rehabilitation + realistic expectations.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: Safety r/t suicide risk + command hallucinations — assess directly, ensure a safe environment, de-escalate, and meet basic needs (nutrition/hydration despite the 'poisoning' delusion)
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Safety (self-harm/command hallucinations + basic needs) comes first. RN role: risk assessment + safe environment + intake.

opts.text: Antipsychotic adverse-effect recognition/management r/t EPS — treat acute dystonia urgently, distinguish akathisia from agitation, and monitor metabolic/QTc parameters
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. EPS (esp. dystonia) can be emergent; correct akathisia handling + metabolic monitoring protect the patient. RN role: monitor + treat adverse effects.

opts.text: Therapeutic communication + alliance r/t psychosis — neither argue with nor reinforce delusions/hallucinations; build trust with calm, consistent, reality-based communication
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. The therapeutic relationship enables assessment, adherence, and recovery. RN role: therapeutic communication.

opts.text: Adherence + medication management r/t relapse prevention — psychoeducation, address barriers/side effects, and consider an LAI
opts.rat: #4 — Significant for long-term outcomes. RN role: adherence support + medication education + LAI consideration.

opts.text: Care coordination + family + community resources r/t recovery — multidisciplinary care, Coordinated Specialty Care, supported education, family psychoeducation, follow-up
opts.rat: #5 — Important throughout + for sustained recovery. RN role: coordinate community-linked, recovery-oriented care + family education.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED for this patient.

opts.text: Assess directly for suicidal ideation and command hallucinations, ensure a safe environment, and offer sealed/packaged foods to maintain intake despite the 'poisoning' delusion
opts.rat: Core safety + basic-needs care. RN role: risk assessment + safe environment + intake.

opts.text: Obtain baseline metabolic parameters + ECG before/with antipsychotic initiation and monitor them periodically
opts.rat: Standard antipsychotic safety monitoring. RN role: baseline + ongoing metabolic/QTc monitoring.

opts.text: Treat acute dystonia urgently with IM/IV benztropine or diphenhydramine and monitor the airway; reassure the frightened patient
opts.rat: Correct management of an EPS emergency. RN role: rapid treatment + airway monitoring + reassurance.

opts.text: Recognize akathisia (restlessness) as a medication effect and manage with dose reduction/switch ± propranolol rather than increasing the antipsychotic
opts.rat: Correct akathisia management. RN role: distinguish from agitation + manage appropriately.

opts.text: Communicate therapeutically — acknowledge the feeling and calmly present reality without arguing or reinforcing the delusions/hallucinations; ask what the voices say
opts.rat: Correct therapeutic communication + command-hallucination assessment. RN role: therapeutic communication.

opts.text: Provide psychoeducation, consider a long-acting injectable to support adherence, and coordinate multidisciplinary + community care (Coordinated Specialty Care, supported education, family psychoeducation)
opts.rat: Adherence + recovery-oriented care coordination. RN role: education + LAI + coordination.

opts.text: Screen periodically with the AIMS for tardive dyskinesia and use the lowest effective antipsychotic dose
opts.rat: Standard TD surveillance + minimization. RN role: AIMS monitoring + dose stewardship.

opts.text: Argue with the patient and present evidence until he admits his delusions are false
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — arguing entrenches delusions and damages trust. Acknowledge the feeling + calmly present reality without confrontation.

opts.text: Increase the antipsychotic dose to treat the patient's restlessness/pacing, assuming worsening psychosis
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — the restlessness is likely akathisia; increasing the dose worsens it. Recognize akathisia and reduce/switch + propranolol.

opts.text: Start clozapine first-line for this new diagnosis because it is the most effective antipsychotic
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia due to agranulocytosis (ANC/REMS), myocarditis, seizures, and constipation; not first-line.

opts.text: Tell the family the illness is caused by their parenting and that the patient just needs to try harder to overcome the negative symptoms
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + harmful — blaming the family and dismissing negative symptoms as effort are inaccurate and increase expressed emotion/relapse. Provide accurate, supportive family psychoeducation.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should act. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Establish safety — assess for suicidal ideation/command hallucinations, ensure a safe low-stimulation environment, and meet basic needs (offer sealed foods/hydration)
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Safety + basic needs come first. RN role: risk assessment + safe environment + intake.

opts.text: Support evaluation + baseline monitoring — facilitate toxicology/TSH/labs, obtain baseline metabolic parameters + ECG before/with antipsychotic initiation
opts.rat: #2 — Second. Exclude mimics + establish safe-prescribing baselines. RN role: workup + baseline monitoring.

opts.text: Administer the antipsychotic and monitor for/treat adverse effects (acute dystonia urgently; recognize akathisia; watch metabolic/QTc)
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Initiate treatment with vigilant adverse-effect monitoring. RN role: administration + EPS/metabolic monitoring.

opts.text: Build the therapeutic alliance with calm, consistent, reality-based communication and provide psychoeducation
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth/ongoing. The alliance enables assessment, adherence, and engagement. RN role: therapeutic communication + education.

opts.text: Coordinate recovery-oriented care — adherence/LAI planning, multidisciplinary + community referrals (Coordinated Specialty Care, supported education), family psychoeducation, and follow-up/relapse-prevention
opts.rat: #5 — Ongoing/discharge. Sustains recovery beyond the admission. RN role: care coordination + family education + follow-up.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each finding, classify the trajectory.

opts.text: Day 1: organic causes excluded, a trusting therapeutic relationship begun (fear acknowledged without arguing the delusions), safety assessed, sealed foods accepted, and an atypical antipsychotic started after baseline labs/ECG
opts.rat: Correct safety + workup + therapeutic engagement + safe medication initiation. Foundation for recovery.

opts.text: Day 3: acute dystonia (torticollis + oculogyric crisis) recognized as an EPS emergency and treated promptly with IM benztropine, with rapid relief and airway monitoring
opts.rat: Prompt recognition + treatment of an EPS emergency prevented harm — correct handling (the event was managed well, protecting the patient).

opts.text: Day 10: positive symptoms improved (delusions less fixed, hallucinations diminished, sleeping/eating), negative symptoms persist as expected, and an LAI + family psychoeducation are planned
opts.rat: Expected medication response (positive > negative) with adherence + recovery planning. Strong trajectory.

opts.text: Discharge: transitioned to Coordinated Specialty Care with an LAI, CBT for psychosis, case management, supported education, metabolic monitoring, family education, and a relapse-prevention/safety plan
opts.rat: Coordinated, recovery-oriented transition that supports adherence and outcomes. Ideal disposition.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — a nurse mistook the patient's akathisia for worsening psychosis and the antipsychotic dose was increased; his restlessness and distress intensified
opts.rat: ⚠️ Mistaking akathisia for agitation and increasing the dose worsened it. Demonstrates why akathisia must be distinguished from psychotic agitation.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — staff repeatedly argued with the patient to prove his delusions were false; he became more guarded, mistrustful, and refused medications
opts.rat: ⚠️ Arguing with delusions damaged trust and adherence. Demonstrates why you neither argue with nor reinforce delusions.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — months later the patient develops involuntary lip-smacking and tongue movements that persist; tardive dyskinesia is identified on AIMS screening
opts.rat: ⚠️ Tardive dyskinesia (late, often irreversible). Demonstrates the importance of AIMS surveillance + minimizing exposure; a VMAT2 inhibitor may be needed.

opts.text: Alternative scenario — after discharge the patient stopped his daily oral antipsychotic (impaired insight, side effects) and relapsed with a return of delusions and hallucinations and rehospitalization
opts.rat: ⚠️ Nonadherence → relapse, the leading cause of rehospitalization. Demonstrates why adherence support + LAIs + coordinated care matter.

opts.text: Early in treatment, the positive symptoms are essentially unchanged in the first day or two before the antipsychotic has reached therapeutic effect, while the patient remains safe and engaged
opts.rat: Antipsychotics take days-to-weeks for positive symptoms to respond; an unchanged status in the first day or two is expected while maintaining safety + engagement, with reassessment as treatment continues.

opts.text: 6 months later (adherent, in Coordinated Specialty Care): positive symptoms in remission, functioning improving, back in classes part-time with support, stable metabolic monitoring
opts.rat: Sustained recovery with adherence + coordinated care + supported education. Standard ideal longer-term outcome.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Psychosocial Integrity, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
